title: Asymptotic Spectrum and Approximation Approaches to Direct-sum Problems
speaker: Jeroen Zuiddam
abstract:
What is the cost of a task if we have to perform it many times? Can we achieve economies of scale? Such "direct-sum problems" play a central role in many areas of mathematics, physics and computer science. Protagonistic problems of this kind are fast matrix multiplication in algebraic complexity theory (Strassen 1969) and Shannon capacity in graph theory (Shannon 1956). Despite much effort, both problems are wide open.
I will give a brief introduction to the asymptotic spectrum duality approach to direct-sum problems, which Strassen originally introduced to study matrix multiplication (asymptotic tensor rank), and which has recently found applications in various areas, including Shannon's capacity (see e.g. the survey Wigderson-Zuiddam 2025).
Next, I will discuss recent approximation (or limit) approaches to problems of this kind. For instance, to determine say the Shannon capacity of a "hard" graph, we construct a sequence of easier to analyse graphs "converging" to it---and similarly for matrix multiplication. What are the right notions of convergence, and how can such sequences be constructed? We will see some examples, including a natural notion of asymptotic spectrum distance arising from Strassen's duality, and another route via polynomials (algebraic geometry).
Based on joint work with David de Boer, Pjotr Buys, Sven Polak, Matthias Christandl, Koen Hoeberechts, Harold Nieuwboer, and Péter Vrana.

proof_stages.label: Direct-sum motivation and two benchmark problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames direct-sum questions as asymptotic growth of a complexity measure under powers, introduces Shannon capacity and fast matrix multiplication as the two guiding problems, and announces approximation by convergent easier objects as the main theme.

proof_stages.label: Shannon-capacity setup and asymptotic-spectrum duality
proof_stages.summary: Graphs are equipped with independence number, strong product, and cohomomorphism order, after which Shannon capacity is identified with the minimum over multiplicative, additive, normalized, monotone spectral points.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic-spectrum distance and continuity of capacity
proof_stages.summary: A sup-norm distance induced by all spectral points is defined, convergence in this distance is shown to force convergence of Shannon capacities, and an equivalent cohomomorphism formulation is given.

proof_stages.label: Fraction-graph convergent sequences
proof_stages.summary: The fraction graphs E_{a/b} are introduced, their cohomomorphism order is shown to mirror the rational order, and deletion/transitivity lemmas prove convergence theorems for rational and irrational limiting labels.

proof_stages.label: Infinite circular graph limit points
proof_stages.summary: Open and closed infinite circular graphs are proposed as limit objects for irrational labels, with distance-zero equivalence in the irrational case and links to unresolved convergence-from-below questions.

proof_stages.label: Finite approximation method and odd-cycle lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: The approximation philosophy is turned into a finite method using nearby auxiliary fraction graphs and orbit-structured independent sets, reproducing known small odd-cycle lower bounds and giving a new lower bound for C15.

proof_stages.label: Tensor rank, asymptotic rank, and Strassen's conjectural picture
proof_stages.summary: Tensor rank and asymptotic rank are introduced as nonmultiplicative analogues of matrix rank, Strassen's tensor-spectrum duality is recalled, and matrix multiplication and the asymptotic-rank conjecture motivate the tensor part.

proof_stages.label: Euclidean limits and Zariski-closed asymptotic-rank strata
proof_stages.summary: For fixed tensor spaces, the set of tensors of asymptotic rank at most r is proved Zariski closed via a closure-of-powers lemma and a polynomial-dimensional span argument, yielding semicontinuity, well-ordering/completeness consequences, and open problems.
